# C284 — Phase 02: Geneva Finance
**Cycle:** C284 | **Phase:** 02 Geneva Finance | **Date:** 2026-04-30
**Output:** `/data/.openclaw/workspace/digitalcoa.ch/private/market_research/phases/ph02_geneva_finance/20260430_phase02_geneva_finance_cycle284.md`

---

## 01 — ICP Language (EN + FR)

### English

**Managing director on the recommendation they cannot decompose:**
"I sign recommendations that go to clients. Some of what goes into those recommendations is generated by AI. I cannot tell a client with evidence what portion came from my analysis versus what the AI system contributed. The recommendation is signed as though it is entirely mine. It is not."

**On the carrier attestation they signed without review:**
"I signed the carrier attestation personally. I understood what I was signing at the time. I do not know what the carrier will ask next renewal cycle. I know the signature is personal. I know the exposure is mine."

**On the institutional client audit that arrived without warning:**
"We received an institutional client's AI governance questionnaire with a ten-business-day deadline. We did not have a process for responding at that speed. We produced answers under pressure. I am not confident the answers were correct. The client will remember how we responded."

**On the nFADP request for an AI-influenced conclusion:**
"A client requested correction of what they described as an inaccurate conclusion in their quarterly report. An AI-generated inference about their risk tolerance. I did not have a methodology for responding to that kind of request. The obligation exists. The response infrastructure does not."

**On the compliance document that may be stale:**
"The compliance documentation we generated eighteen months ago when we adopted AI tools — is it still accurate? I have no version tracking. I have no mechanism for determining whether the text we are using reflects current regulatory requirements."

### Français

**Directeur général sur la recommandation qu'il ne peut pas décomposer:**
"Je signe des recommandations qui partent aux clients. Une partie de ce qui entre dans ces recommandations est générée par l'IA. Je ne peux pas expliquer à un client avec des preuves quelle partie provient de mon analyse versus ce que le système d'IA a contribué. La recommandation est signée comme si elle était entièrement la mienne. Elle ne l'est pas."

**Sur l'attestation carrier qu'il a signée sans révision:**
"J'ai signé l'attestation carrier personnellement. J'ai compris ce que je signais à ce moment-là. Je ne sais pas ce que le carrier demandera lors du prochain cycle de renouvellement. Je sais que la signature est personnelle. Je sais que l'exposition est la mienne."

**Sur l'audit client institutionnel arrivé sans préavis:**
"Nous avons reçu un questionnaire de gouvernance IA d'un client institutionnel avec un délai de dix jours ouvrables. Nous n'avions pas de processus pour répondre à cette vitesse. Nous avons produit des réponses sous pression. Je ne suis pas confiant que les réponses étaient correctes. Le client se souviendra de comment nous avons répondu."

**Sur la demande nFADP pour une conclusion influencée par l'IA:**
"Un client a demandé la correction de ce qu'il décrivait comme une conclusion inexacte dans son rapport trimestriel. Une inférence générée par l'IA sur sa tolérance au risque. Je n'avais pas de méthodologie pour répondre à ce type de demande. L'obligation existe. L'infrastructure de réponse n'existe pas."

**Sur le document de conformité qui peut être obsolète:**
"La documentation de conformité que nous avons générée il y a dix-huit mois lors de l'adoption des outils d'IA — est-elle toujours exacte? Je n'ai pas de suivi de version. Je n'ai pas de mécanisme pour déterminer si le texte que nous utilisons reflète les exigences réglementaires actuelles."

---

## 02 — Market Signals

**Signal 1: AI authorship attribution as primary accountability question at boutique scale**
The MD who signs recommendations cannot document what portion is professional judgment versus AI contribution. The signed recommendation represents an attestation that is structurally non-separable from AI content.

**Signal 2: Carrier attestation renewal creating personal liability against undefined standard**
Each renewal cycle adds new content the signatory evaluates for the first time at signature. The carrier's legal team wrote the language to protect carrier interests. The MD has not had the language analyzed against personal exposure.

**Signal 3: Institutional client questionnaire response as unmanaged operational event**
Ten-business-day deadlines designed for large institutional compliance functions. Boutique firms have no response infrastructure. The response under pressure creates a record the client will reference against future expectations.

**Signal 4: nFADP Article 15 and 21 response protocol entirely absent for AI-influenced decisions**
Client rights requests arriving for AI-influenced recommendations before any response protocol exists. The protocol requires identifying which conclusions were AI-generated, evaluating accuracy against client ground truth, and providing explanation of automated decision logic — all without methodology.

**Signal 5: AI-generated compliance documentation as unversioned regulatory text**
Compliance documents generated at adoption are in active use with no version tracking. Whether they remain accurate against current requirements cannot be determined. The staleness is invisible until regulatory examination surfaces the gap.

**Signal 6: Personal attestation language written by carrier legal team**
The MD signs personal attestation commitments defined by language the carrier's legal team wrote. The exposure is defined by language the MD did not negotiate and has not analyzed against Swiss professional liability doctrine.

---

## 03 — Competitive Analysis

**Gap 1: AI authorship attribution methodology for client-facing recommendations**
No Geneva service offers methodology for documenting what portion of an AI-assisted recommendation is attributable to MD professional judgment versus AI contribution. The documentation required is not "describe AI's role" but document specific attribution of professional accountability when contributions are architecturally non-disaggregable.

**Gap 2: Carrier attestation pre-signing review from signatory's personal liability perspective**
No Geneva service offers pre-signing review of carrier attestation language against the signatory's personal exposure under Swiss professional liability doctrine. The review would need to analyze what the language commits the signatory to personally.

**Gap 3: Institutional client AI questionnaire response infrastructure**
No service offers pre-built response infrastructure for institutional client AI governance questionnaires designed for boutique firms — templates, internal routing, quality review, timeline management — so the firm is not improvising under pressure.

**Gap 4: nFADP Article 15/21 response protocol for AI-influenced decisions**
No Geneva service offers a specific response protocol for Article 15 accuracy correction and Article 21 automated decision explanation requests when the decisions were AI-influenced.

**Gap 5: AI compliance document version management system**
No Geneva service offers a compliance document version management system that attaches regulatory change monitoring to AI-generated compliance text.

**Gap 6: Carrier attestation evolution tracking as anticipatory intelligence**
No Geneva service offers systematic tracking of carrier attestation iteration patterns across renewal cycles to anticipate next signature requirements.

---

## 04 — Compliance Context

**FINMA Guidance 08/2024 and AI inventory obligations:**
FINMA formalized AI inventory requirements. The boundary between reportable AI and non-reportable digital tool is undefined at boutique scale.

**ASIP two-layer accountability:**
AI systems introduce complexity into both asset management and advisory layers that existing accountability frameworks do not address.

**SFAMA FIDLEG Article 26 proportionate methodology absence:**
No proportionate methodology exists for FIDLEG Article 26 compliance at boutique scale. The absence means boutique firms cannot confirm compliance.

**nFADP Articles 6, 15, and 21:**
AI-inferred client conclusions activate Article 15 accuracy obligations. Article 21 AI decision documentation obligations accumulate without methodology. Article 6 lawful basis questions activate for data processed through AI systems.

**CoE AI Convention present-tense obligations:**
The AI Convention is in force. Obligations activate through existing client mandates before implementing legislation.

**ISSB S2 and AI electricity disclosure:**
Whether AI-assisted portfolio construction creates reportable Scope 2 electricity consumption obligation. No Geneva-specific methodology exists.

---

## 05 — Lead Magnet Draft

### English

**Geneva Wealth Manager AI Governance Briefing: The Documentation That Survives the Questions That Are Already Arriving**

Managing directors at Geneva wealth management firms are encountering AI governance obligations that arrive before the documentation frameworks exist to address them. This briefing is structured around the specific structural gaps creating exposure at boutique scale.

**Who this is for:** Managing directors and senior advisors at Geneva-based boutique wealth management firms who use AI systems for client reporting, portfolio analysis, and compliance documentation.

**What the briefing covers:**

- Your carrier attestation: what you are personally committing to when you sign, and what the carrier's language commits you to that you may not have analyzed
- AI authorship attribution: what you can and cannot document about what in your signed recommendations came from your professional judgment versus what the AI contributed
- Institutional client questionnaires: how to respond when the timeline is calibrated to institutional compliance functions, not boutique firms
- nFADP Article 15 and 21 requests: what to do when a client requests correction of an AI-influenced conclusion or explanation of automated decision logic
- AI compliance document staleness: how to determine whether the compliance documentation you generated eighteen months ago still reflects current regulatory requirements
- The documentation you should have built by now

**Format:** 90-minute secure video or in-person briefing, in English or French
**Investment:** CHF 2,800 per firm
**Output:** Written summary within 48 hours, covering your firm's specific situation and the three most urgent actions

I work with a small number of Geneva wealth management firms on their specific AI governance situations. This briefing is structured around your firm's actual configuration, not a generic framework.

To schedule: reply to this message or use the contact form on digitalcoa.ch.

---

### Français

**Briefing de Gouvernance IA pour Gestionnaires de Patrimoine Genevois: La Documentation Qui Survit Aux Questions Qui Arrivent Déjà**

Les directeurs généraux de cabinets de gestion de patrimoine genevois rencontrent des obligations de gouvernance de l'IA qui arrivent avant que les cadres de documentation n'existent pour les adresser. Ce briefing est structuré autour des écarts structurels spécifiques créant une exposition à l'échelle boutiquarde.

**Pour qui c'est:** Directeurs généraux et conseillers seniors de cabinets de gestion de patrimoine genevois basés qui utilisent des systèmes d'IA pour les rapports clients, l'analyse de portefeuille et la documentation de conformité.

**Ce que couvre le briefing:**

- Votre attestation de porteur: ce que vous vous engagez personnellement à faire quand vous signez, et ce que le langage du porteur vous engage à faire que vous n'avez peut-être pas analysé
- L'attribution de paternité de l'IA: ce que vous pouvez et ne pouvez pas documenter à propos de ce qui dans vos recommandations signées provenait de votre jugement professionnel versus ce que l'IA a contribué
- Les questionnaires clients institutionnels: comment répondre quand le délai est calibré pour les fonctions de conformité institutionnelles, pas pour les cabinets boutiquards
- Les demandes nFADP Art. 15 et 21: quoi faire quand un client demande la correction d'une conclusion influencée par l'IA ou l'explication de la logique de décision automatisée
- La stalesse des documents de conformité IA: comment déterminer si la documentation de conformité que vous avez générée il y a dix-huit mois reflète encore les exigences réglementaires actuelles
- La documentation que vous auriez dû construire maintenant

**Format:** Briefing sécurisé de 90 minutes par vidéo ou en personne, en anglais ou en français
**Investissement:** CHF 2 800 par cabinet
**Livrable:** Résumé écrit dans les 48 heures, couvrant la situation spécifique de votre cabinet et les trois actions les plus urgentes

Je travaille avec un nombre limité de cabinets de gestion de patrimoine genevois sur leurs situations spécifiques de gouvernance de l'IA. Ce briefing est structuré autour de la configuration réelle de votre cabinet, pas d'un cadre générique.

Pour planifier: répondez à ce message ou utilisez le formulaire de contact sur digitalcoa.ch.

## 06 — Findings Summary

**Finding 1: AI authorship attribution as structurally non-separable exposure in signed recommendations**
The MD signs recommendations that include AI-generated content. The AI contribution cannot be separated from human contribution. The signature covers both. This is not a disclosure problem — it is an authorship problem with structural non-separation that cannot be disaggregated retroactively.

**Finding 2: Carrier attestation language written by carrier legal team creating undefined personal exposure**
The carrier's legal team wrote attestation language to protect carrier interests. The MD signs personal commitments defined by that language without analysis against Swiss professional liability doctrine. The undefined operative standard is the specific mechanism creating personal exposure.

**Finding 3: Institutional client questionnaire response timeline incompatibility as unmanaged operational event**
Institutional client deadlines calibrated to large institutional compliance functions. Boutique firms have no response infrastructure. The response under pressure creates a record the client will reference against future expectations. Named as unmanaged event, not just gap.

**Finding 4: nFADP Article 15 and 21 response protocol absence for AI-influenced decisions**
Client rights requests arriving for AI-influenced recommendations before any response protocol exists. The protocol requires identifying AI-generated conclusions, evaluating accuracy against client ground truth, and providing automated decision explanations — all without methodology. Named as structural absence.

**Finding 5: AI-generated compliance document version staleness as invisible present-tense exposure**
Compliance documents generated at adoption are in active use with no version tracking. Whether they remain accurate against current requirements cannot be determined. The staleness is invisible until regulatory examination surfaces the gap. Named as present-tense exposure.

**Finding 6: Carrier attestation evolution tracking as anticipatory intelligence function**
Each renewal cycle adds new content the signatory evaluates at signature for the first time. Systematic tracking of carrier iteration patterns across renewal cycles would allow anticipatory positioning before the next signature requirement arrives. Unnamed in Geneva market as discrete intelligence function.
